Even without discussing emotion, you can talk about stability: - Does C feel like the point of rest (arrival) and Am like a detour? - Or does Am feel like the resting point and C like a color shift? Starting the loop on Am (vi–IV–I–V) plus melodic emphasis on A often tips perception toward A as center, even if no G# appears. 5) Chord-scale behavior and tendency tones In C major, B tends to resolve to C (leading tone to tonic). In A minor, G# tends to resolve to A. If your melodic lines frequently treat B→C as structural resolution, that’s C major behavior. If they treat G#→A as structural resolution, that’s A minor behavior. 6) Analytical compromise: “relative minor tonicization” vs “mixture of centers” Sometimes the best description is: - “The loop is in C major but with vi as a prolonged tonic-function area,” or - “The music is centric on A (Aeolian) using the C-major collection,” without claiming a full modulation. If you tell me whether the loop ever uses E major/E7 or G#, and where melodic phrases tend to settle, I can give a more confident label.
Can we talk about why the progression is so compatible with a wide range of melodies? Like, is there something about the scale degrees it highlights?

Yes—one reason it’s so melody-friendly is that across the four chords, most scale degrees become available as either chord tones or very stable extensions at different moments, and several tones persist as common tones. In C major with C–G–Am–F: Chord tones by scale degree: - I (C–E–G): 1, 3, 5 - V (G–B–D): 5, 7, 2 - vi (A–C–E): 6, 1, 3 - IV (F–A–C): 4, 6, 1 Across the loop you cover: 1,2,3,4,5,6,7 — all diatonic degrees show up as chord tones somewhere. That’s huge. 1) The “anchor” degrees: 1, 3, 6 Scale degree 1 (C) is in I, vi, IV. Scale degree 3 (E) is in I and vi. Scale degree 6 (A) is in vi and IV. So a melody that leans heavily on 1/3/6 can sound consonant for most of the loop. 2) Smooth non-chord tones Even when a melody uses a note that isn’t a chord tone at a given moment, it often functions as: - a stepwise passing tone, - a suspension, - a neighbor tone, and resolves quickly to a chord tone that’s available in the next chord. Example: scale degree 2 (D) - Over C (I), D is a 9th (a stable-ish color in pop). - Over G (V), D is a chord tone (5th of G). - Over Am (vi), D is a 11th (can pass to C or E). - Over F (IV), D is a 6th (again a common pop color). So even “non-chord” tones can be treated as consonant extensions depending on style. 3) The loop creates multiple “safe” melodic targets Because every scale degree is a chord tone somewhere, a melody can create motivic consistency by repeating a note and letting the harmony recontextualize it. Example: hold E across chords: - Over C: E is 3rd (stable) - Over G: E is 13th (color) - Over Am: E is 5th (stable) - Over F: E is major 7 (color, slightly tense) That single pitch can carry a whole line if handled carefully. 4) Avoiding hard dissonance by design The progression avoids certain harsh clashes that would happen if, say, you had chords with more chromatic tendency tones or altered dominants. In pure diatonic triads, most clashes are mild and resolvable stepwise. 5) Bass motion supports scalar melodies Roots move by fourth/fifth or step (C→G is a fifth; G→A is a step; A→F is a third; F→C is a fourth). This supports melodies that move stepwise without constantly colliding with bass. If you want, give a short melody (just note names or scale degrees) and I can show which notes are chord tones over each chord and where you’d treat notes as suspensions/passing tones.
Let’s analyze a generic melody: 3–2–1–2 over the four chords in C major. How does that sit against C–G–Am–F?

Using scale degrees in C major: - 3 = E - 2 = D - 1 = C Assume one melody note per chord (a common analytical simplification): Chord 1: C (I) with melody 3 (E) - E is the 3rd of the chord: consonant, stable. Chord 2: G (V) with melody 2 (D) - D is the 5th of the chord (G–B–D): consonant, stable. Chord 3: Am (vi) with melody 1 (C) - C is the 3rd of Am (A–C–E): consonant, stable. Chord 4: F (IV) with melody 2 (D) - D is not a chord tone in F (F–A–C). It’s the 6th (13th) above F. In many pop/tonal contexts, 6 over IV is a gentle color tone; it can function as: - a consonant-sounding added 6 (F6: F–A–C–D), or - a suspension/passing tone resolving to C or E when harmony returns to I. Now look at the melodic contour 3–2–1–2: E–D–C–D is stepwise and naturally suggests continuation. Harmonically: - The first three notes are chord tones—very grounded. - The last note adds mild color on IV, which can be satisfying because IV is already “open” and often supports added tones. If the loop repeats back to C (I), that final D can resolve down to C at the top of the next cycle (2→1), creating a clean resolution across the seam. So this is a textbook example of why the loop is melody-friendly: you can write a simple scalar idea and it lands on stable chord tones most of the time, with tasteful color at the end. If you want to increase tension, swap the last 2 (D) for 7 (B) over F: that would be a tritone-ish tendency relative to F (as #11 in the key context) and would want to resolve. Or keep 2 but make it a suspension that resolves on I.
What about the famous “four chords” idea where the same melody can fit over different progressions? Is I–V–vi–IV kind of a template that tolerates reharmonization?

Yes, it’s unusually tolerant of reharmonization because the progression’s pitch content overlaps heavily with the diatonic scale and because many melodic tones can be reinterpreted as chord tones/extensions across multiple chords. Two key concepts explain this: 1) Diatonic saturation As we saw, across I, V, vi, IV you get all seven scale degrees as chord tones somewhere. That means a melody that stays mostly diatonic can be harmonized by choosing, at each moment, a chord that contains the melody note (or supports it as an extension). 2) Chord-tone reinterpretation (function of a note changes) A single melody pitch can serve different roles depending on chord: Example pitch: C (scale degree 1) - Over C: root - Over Am: 3rd - Over F: 5th - Over Dm (ii): 7th (color) - Over G: 11th (color) So if your melody emphasizes C a lot, you can rotate between chords that “legitimize” it. Why I–V–vi–IV is a particularly strong template: - It includes both tonic and dominant (I and V), which makes it sound “complete.” - It includes two chords that share many tones with I (vi and IV), which makes substitutions easy. - It provides a common pop cadence seam (IV→I or V→I depending on rotation), which helps loops feel coherent. Typical reharmonization moves around it: - Replace vi with iii (in C: Em) for a lighter tonic-substitute color. - Replace IV with ii (Dm) for a more predominant feel. - Replace V with V7 or add suspensions (Gsus4→G) to increase pull. - Use inversions to adjust bass while keeping upper structure similar. So yes: it’s a template not because it’s harmonically “empty,” but because it’s harmonically *generous*—it offers many stable intersections with typical diatonic melodies. If you provide a short melody (even 8 notes), I can show at least two different four-chord loops that harmonize it while keeping a similar overall feel.

I want to understand the role of inversions and bass lines here, but without thinking about any specific arrangement. How do inversions change the perceived progression when the Roman numerals stay the same?

Inversions can change perceived function, momentum, and “direction” because the bass note is a primary cue for harmonic stability and for how one chord leads to the next—even if the chord identity (Roman numeral) is unchanged. Think of a chord as having: - root (functional identity), - bass (grounding/line), - upper voices (color and tendency tones). Changing inversion changes the bass line while keeping the same pitch set. 1) Bass as a structural melody In C–G–Am–F, root-position bass is C–G–A–F. That line has a distinctive shape (down a fifth, up a step, down a third). If you invert chords to create a stepwise bass, you can make the progression feel smoother and sometimes less “sectional.” Example: choose inversions for stepwise bass: C (root): bass C G (1st inv): bass B Am (1st inv): bass C F (2nd inv): bass C This yields bass C–B–C–C, which reduces the sense of harmonic “travel” and emphasizes a static center. Another option: C (root): bass C G (2nd inv): bass D Am (1st inv): bass C F (1st inv): bass A Bass C–D–C–A: more scalar, with a clear descent at the end. 2) Inversions can weaken or strengthen functional cues Dominant function is often reinforced when the root of V is in the bass (G in bass in C major). Put V in first inversion (B in bass), and the dominant feel can be softened, because the bass no longer presents scale degree 5 as the foundation. Similarly, IV in second inversion (C in bass) can sound like a tonic prolongation with added tones rather than a clear subdominant move, because the bass is scale degree 1. So even though the Roman numerals are “V” and “IV,” the *felt* function can shift: - V6 can sound like a passing harmony connecting I to vi. - IV64 can sound like tonic with embellishment. 3) Inversions shape voice-leading tension Some inversions create stronger tendency-tone resolutions: - In G/B (V6), the bass note B is the leading tone and tends to resolve to C—very directional. If the next chord has C in the bass, you get an obvious resolution in the lowest voice, which can feel cadential even if the chord progression isn’t a textbook cadence. 4) Inversions can imply different contrapuntal frameworks Pop loops often behave like “upper-structure harmony over a bass line.” If the bass line becomes stepwise, the harmony can feel more like contrapuntal motion than block-chord progression. So: Roman numerals label chord roots, but inversions rewrite the bass narrative, and that can change how the ear parses stability, motion, and arrival. If you tell me what kind of bass contour you want (stepwise, arpeggiated, mostly static, etc.), I can suggest an inversion scheme that preserves I–V–vi–IV but changes the perceived drive.
Could we connect this to modes? Sometimes people say the vi–IV–I–V loop sounds “modal” or “Aeolian.” Is that accurate if there’s no raised 7?

It can be accurate in a descriptive sense, but it depends on what you mean by “modal.” If vi–IV–I–V in C major is Am–F–C–G, and you never use G#, then the pitch collection is the same as C major (Ionian) and A natural minor (Aeolian). Calling it “Aeolian” is often shorthand for “A is acting as a center using the natural-minor collection.” Here’s a clearer breakdown: 1) Collection vs. center - Collection: the set of notes used. Here it’s {A, B, C, D, E, F, G}. - Center (tonic): the pitch that feels like home. You can use the “C-major collection” while centering A; that yields A Aeolian as a *mode* of the major scale. So if Am feels like the gravitational center (through repetition, beginnings/endings, melodic emphasis), then “Aeolian” is a reasonable label even without G#. 2) Why it can sound modal In minor keys in common-practice tonality, harmony often uses harmonic minor (raised 7) to create a strong dominant (E major in A minor). If you avoid that and keep G natural, you avoid the leading-tone pull to A. That weakens classical minor-key dominant function and can produce a more “modal” sense of minor: less directed, more cyclical. In other words: it’s not that the chords are exotic; it’s that the lack of leading-tone dominant makes it less like functional minor and more like a mode-based center. 3) But it can also be heard as C major with tonic substitution If C still feels like home, then Am is simply vi in C major, not “Aeolian.” The same loop can be heard either way depending on melodic and phrase cues. 4) A practical test - If the melody frequently resolves to A and treats A as final, you’ll likely hear Aeolian centricity. - If the melody resolves to C and treats C as final, you’ll likely hear Ionian centricity with vi coloration. So: “Aeolian” can be accurate as a centric/modal description, especially when the loop starts on vi and the melody supports A as center, and especially when there’s no raised 7 creating functional minor dominants.
Now, could you explain how secondary dominants interact with this progression? Like, if I wanted to add V/V or something, where would it fit and what would it do?

In C major, V/V is the dominant of V. Since V is G, the dominant of G is D (major), often D7 (D–F#–A–C). Adding it introduces chromaticism (F#) and increases directional pull. Where it can fit in I–V–vi–IV (C–G–Am–F): 1) Insert V/V before V Basic idea: approach G with its dominant. C → D7 → G → Am → F Roman numerals: I → V/V → V → vi → IV Effect: - Stronger drive into the G chord. - The F# (raised 4 in C major) is a bright tendency tone that wants to resolve to G. - It briefly tonicizes G (makes it feel like a temporary tonic). 2) Use V/vi before vi (tonicize the relative minor) In C major, vi is Am. The dominant of Am is E (major), often E7 (E–G#–B–D). C → G → E7 → Am → F Roman numerals: I → V → V/vi → vi → IV Effect: - Stronger pull into Am, making vi feel more like a true minor tonic (at least locally). - Introduces G# (raised 5 in C major), which is a very strong signal of A minor tonicization. 3) Use V/IV before IV IV is F. Dominant of F is C (major), often C7 if you want a stronger pull to F (C–E–G–Bb). C → G → Am → C7 → F Roman numerals: I → V → vi → V/IV → IV Effect: - Strengthens the move into IV. - Bb introduces modal mixture/borrowed color (since Bb is not in C major). 4) Replacing V with a secondary dominant chain You can create a circle-of-fifths intensification: C → A7 → D7 → G → Am → F (then back) Here A7 is V/V/V (dominant of D), D7 is V/V, etc. Effect: a more “jazz/pop” directed motion while still landing on the familiar chords. General principle: - Secondary dominants increase functional direction and reduce the “axis” feel (more goal-directed). - They also clarify temporary centers (tonicization), which can make the harmony feel less purely cyclic. If you tell me whether you prefer minimal chromaticism (just one secondary dominant) or a more extended chain, I can suggest a version that still loops cleanly back to I without sounding like it changed songs.
When you add those chromatic notes like F# or G#, how do you keep the melody from clashing? Is it mostly about targeting chord tones?

Mostly, yes: when harmony introduces chromatic tendency tones, the safest strategy is to make the melody either (a) align with the altered chord tones at that moment, or (b) avoid the altered pitch class entirely by choosing stable common tones or consonant extensions. A few concrete tactics: 1) Melody as chord-tone guide at the chromatic chord If you insert D7 (V/V) in C major (D–F#–A–C): - Strong melody targets: D (root), F# (3rd), A (5th), C (7th). - If the melody hits F natural at that moment, it clashes hard against F# (minor 9 / augmented unison relationship). So either: - move melody to F#, - or choose a different note like A or C that fits D7. 2) Use common tones across the chromatic move Example: C → D7 → G - Common tone between C major (C–E–G) and D7 (D–F#–A–C) is C. A melody sustaining C across C and D7 can sound smooth, because C becomes the 7th of D7, which has a strong tendency to resolve down to B over G. So you can “hide” the chromaticism in inner voices while the melody sits on a common tone. 3) Treat chromatic tones as passing/neighbor tones in melody If the harmony introduces F#, the melody can approach it stepwise and leave it stepwise, making it feel like a directed tendency tone rather than an arbitrary clash. 4) Avoid the altered pitch class by emphasizing stable scale degrees Over D7, if you avoid F/F# entirely and sing A–G–A or C–B–C, you can still sound consonant-ish because: - A is chord tone (5th of D7). - C is chord tone (7th of D7). - Those notes also connect well to the following G chord. 5) Use suspensions that resolve with the chord change Example: melody holds G over D7 (G is the 11th over D), then resolves to F# or to G’s chord tone after the resolution to G. Depending on style, that can be acceptable if treated as a suspension. General guideline: - The more chromatic the harmony, the more important it is that the melody either participates in the chromatic logic (tendency-tone resolution) or stays on tones that remain stable across the change (common tones, 3rds/7ths that resolve). If you want, pick one insertion (D7 before G, or E7 before Am) and we can list “safe melody notes” and “high-risk notes” over that chord in C major.
Let’s do E7 before Am. In C major, that’s a pretty strong color. What are safe melody notes over E7, and how would they resolve?

E7 is E–G#–B–D. In the context of C major, it’s V/vi, tonicizing A minor. Safe melody notes (chord tones) and typical resolutions: 1) E (root of E7) - Stable over E7. - Common resolution: E can go to E (common tone) or to C over Am (E is 5th of Am; C is 3rd). - Because E is also in the C-major collection, it doesn’t scream “outside” even though the chord does. 2) G# (3rd of E7) — the standout chromatic note - Very strong tendency to resolve upward to A (leading tone in A minor). - If the next chord is Am, melody G#→A is the clearest “this is V of A” signal. - Another option is G#→G natural if you *don’t* go to Am (but in this case you are going to Am, so G#→A is the functional resolution). 3) B (5th of E7) - Can resolve to: - C (step up) over Am (C is chord tone), - A (down a whole step) if you want a leapy resolution, - or stay on B if the next harmony accommodates it (Am doesn’t include B, so if B sustains into Am it becomes a 9th—often acceptable as a color). 4) D (7th of E7) - Classical tendency: resolves downward to C# if going to A major, but here we’re going to A minor. - In E7 → Am, D often resolves down to C (a stable chord tone in Am) or can stay as D as a 11th over Am (less stable, but workable). - A very functional voice-leading is D→C while G#→A simultaneously. Non-chord tones that can work as extensions (style-dependent): - F# is the 9th of E7: can resolve to G# or E, but it introduces yet another chromatic pitch. - A is the 11th of E7: it clashes with G# (as a minor 2nd) if both are prominent; it can work as a suspension if it resolves to G# before the chord resolves, but it’s riskier. High-risk melody notes over E7 in this key context: - G natural: clashes with G# (minor 2nd) and undermines the dominant-of-A signal. - C natural: not in E7; it’s a b13 relative to E and can sound quite tense. It can be used deliberately, but it’s not “safe.” Resolution patterns that clearly express the tonicization: - G# → A (leading tone resolution) - D → C (7th resolves down) - B → C (5th to 3rd of Am) or B → A So a very “clean” melodic move is: G#–A on the E7→Am change, optionally with D–C in another voice or as a melodic motion too. If you want, we can take a diatonic melody and show the minimal edits needed to accommodate E7 (typically sharpening G to G# at that moment).
This makes me think about tendency tones. In I–V–vi–IV without any chromatic chords, what are the key tendency tones, and where do they resolve?

In diatonic C major with triads only, tendency tones are milder than in a dominant seventh context, but they still exist—especially around scale degree 7 (B) and scale degree 4 (F) depending on harmonic context. For C–G–Am–F: 1) Scale degree 7 (B) in the V chord - In G major (G–B–D), B is the leading tone to C (scale degree 1). - In classical V→I, B resolves up to C. - In this progression, V often goes to vi (G→Am). In that case: - B can resolve to C (still stepwise up), and C is a chord tone in Am (the 3rd). So the leading-tone tendency is still satisfied even though the harmony “deceives” to vi instead of I. That’s a big reason the deceptive move works smoothly: the leading tone still resolves correctly. 2) Scale degree 2 (D) in the V chord - D often resolves to C or E depending on context. - In V→I, D can resolve to C (as 9–8 if treated as a suspension) or to E (as 2→3). - In V→vi, D can resolve to E (chord tone of Am) or to C (also chord tone). Again, easy. 3) Scale degree 4 (F) and its tendency toward E (in a tonic context) In C major, F is scale degree 4 and often resolves down to E when it functions as a suspension over I (4–3). In this loop, F appears as: - Root of IV (F major), where it’s stable. - But when you move IV→I (F→C), the note F can resolve to E in an upper voice, providing that classic 4–3 resolution flavor even though the chord motion is plagal. So IV→I can contain a “hidden” tendency-tone resolution: F→E. 4) Scale degree 6 (A) tends to move to G (common in IV→I or vi→IV contexts) Not a strict tendency tone like leading tone, but A often moves stepwise to G in voice-leading: - In F→C, A→G is a smooth step that helps closure. 5) The deceptive cadence preserves tendency-tone logic The hallmark of deceptive resolution is: V’s tendency tones resolve as if to I, but the bass/root goes somewhere else. In V→vi: - B→C (leading tone resolution) - D→E (often) - G can move to A (stepwise) So you get the sense of resolution without the expected tonic root. If you want, I can write a “tendency-tone resolution skeleton” for the progression: choose one note from each chord (like 7→1, 2→3, 5→6) and show how it creates a consistent inner-voice line across the loop.
Yes, do that. Give me two inner-voice lines that outline the progression’s logic.

Here are two useful inner-voice lines (in scale degrees and note names) that track common tendency resolutions across C–G–Am–F. Progression: C (I) → G (V) → Am (vi) → F (IV) → back to C (I) Inner line 1: 7→1 resolution embedded in V→vi and IV→I Let’s place B in the V chord and resolve it to C in the vi chord: - Over C (I): you can start on C (1) or D (2) as a preparation; I’ll choose C (1). - Over G (V): move to B (7). (C→B is a step down.) - Over Am (vi): resolve B→C (7→1). C is chord tone (3rd of Am). - Over F (IV): keep C as a common tone (C is 5th of F). - Back to C (I): keep C (root). So line: C–B–C–C–C (scale degrees 1–7–1–1–1) It’s simple, but it highlights the key “leading tone resolves even in deceptive motion” idea. Inner line 2: 2→3 and 4→3 resolutions (dominant and plagal flavors) Let’s create a line that emphasizes D→E (2→3) and F→E (4→3) around the seam. One possibility: - Over C (I): start on E (3). - Over G (V): move to D (2). (E→D step.) - Over Am (vi): resolve D→E (2→3). E is chord tone (5th of Am). - Over F (IV): move E→F (3→4) as a preparation. - Back to C (I): resolve F→E (4→3) at the seam. So line: E–D–E–F–E (scale degrees 3–2–3–4–3) This line is nice because it shows: - dominant-related resolution (2→3) when moving V→vi, - and a classic suspension-like resolution (4→3) when IV→I. You can run both lines simultaneously as two inner voices: - Line 1: C–B–C–C–C - Line 2: E–D–E–F–E Together they imply chord tones at each step and make the progression feel inevitable through stepwise voice-leading. If you’d like, I can generate a third line focusing on 5→6→6→5 (G→A→A→G) which outlines the deceptive lift from V to vi.
Do the third line too, and then show how these lines correspond to actual chord voicings at each step.

Third inner-voice line focusing on 5→6 motion: Line 3: G–G–A–A–G (scale degrees 5–5–6–6–5) - Over C (I): G is the 5th (stable). - Over G (V): G is the root (stable). - Over Am (vi): A is the root (stable). - Over F (IV): A is the 3rd (stable). - Back to C (I): G is the 5th (stable). Now, combine the three lines into chordal snapshots. We’ll treat them as three voices (low/mid/high unspecified), and ensure each vertical slice matches the chord. Progression chords: 1) C major (C–E–G) Lines at chord 1: - Line 1: C - Line 2: E - Line 3: G Vertical: C–E–G = C major (complete triad) 2) G major (G–B–D) Lines at chord 2: - Line 1: B - Line 2: D - Line 3: G Vertical: B–D–G = G major in first inversion (G/B) 3) A minor (A–C–E) Lines at chord 3: - Line 1: C - Line 2: E - Line 3: A Vertical: C–E–A = A minor in first inversion (Am/C) 4) F major (F–A–C) Lines at chord 4: - Line 1: C - Line 2: F - Line 3: A Vertical: C–F–A = F major in second inversion (F/C) 5) Back to C (C–E–G) Lines at chord 5 (repeat): - Line 1: C - Line 2: E - Line 3: G Vertical: C–E–G = C major So these three lines literally generate the smooth close-position voicing chain we found earlier: C (root) → G6 → Am6 → F64 → C This is a neat demonstration: the progression’s “inevitability” can be reduced to three stepwise (or stationary) lines that each obey simple tendency-tone behavior: - B resolves to C, - D resolves to E, - F resolves to E across the seam, - G lifts to A at the deceptive move. If you want to explore variations, we can keep the same three lines but swap one chord (e.g., use Em instead of Am) and see how the lines adapt.
That’s helpful. Now, how does all of this relate to the idea of “tonic prolongation” in pop harmony? Is I–V–vi–IV basically prolonging I?

It often can be heard that way, especially when inversions or melodic emphasis keep C (scale degree 1) salient through most of the loop. “Tonic prolongation” means the music stays within an expanded tonic area even while chords change, because the non-tonic chords function more as embellishments than as genuine departures requiring cadential return. How I–V–vi–IV can prolong I: 1) Shared tones with I - vi (Am) shares C and E with I (C major). - IV (F) shares C with I and is closely related. These chords can feel like neighboring harmonies around tonic rather than true modulatory moves. 2) Inversion choices can keep tonic in the bass As we saw, you can voice: C (C–E–G) G/B (B–D–G) Am/C (C–E–A) F/C (C–F–A) Here, C is in the bass for 3 out of 4 chords (C, Am/C, F/C). That makes the whole loop feel like a tonic pedal/anchor, with upper voices shifting. In that reading, the “progression” is less about roots and more about upper-voice motion over a stable tonal center. 3) Dominant becomes a passing intensification Even V can be heard as a passing or neighboring event if: - it’s inverted (G/B), - it’s short in duration, - and it resolves by stepwise voice-leading rather than by a strong V→I cadence. 4) But it’s not always tonic prolongation If V is in root position and emphasized, and if the melody uses leading-tone behavior prominently, then the dominant function is more than embellishment—it’s real tension, even if it resolves deceptively. So whether the loop is “basically prolonging I” depends on: - bass behavior (does it keep returning to C or dwell on C?), - melodic centricity (does the melody treat C as a stable goal?), - phrase boundaries (do phrases end on I?), - and how strongly V is articulated. A balanced view: - The loop can be analyzed both as a functional cycle (T–D–TS–PD) and as tonic prolongation with embellishing harmonies (especially with specific inversion/voice-leading choices). Pop analysis often toggles between these lenses. If you want, we can do a Schenker-ish reduction (without going full classical) where C is prolonged and the other chords are neighboring/passing harmonies supporting stepwise lines.
I’d like a reduction idea, but keep it accessible. Show how the loop can be reduced to a simple I with embellishments, and then contrast that with a functional reading.

Accessible reduction of C–G–Am–F: A) Tonic-prolongation (embellishment) reading Goal: show that scale degree 1 (C) is continuously supported, and the upper voices make small motions. One way: - Treat C as a prolonged tonic “support tone.” - Hear the other chords as ways of harmonizing stepwise inner lines around C. Using the smooth inversion chain: C: C–E–G G/B: B–D–G Am/C: C–E–A F/C: C–F–A Notice: - C is present in 3 of 4 chords, and can be held as a common tone in an inner voice or bass. - The upper structure moves by step: - E→D→E→F→E (one line) - C→B→C→C→C (another line) - G→G→A→A→G (another line) So you can reduce the loop to: - Prolonged I (C) with neighboring motions that decorate it: - Dominant-color neighbor (G/B) introduces B and D as upper neighbors/lower neighbors to C and E. - vi (Am/C) introduces A as a neighboring/supporting tone to G. - IV (F/C) introduces F as an upper neighbor to E, then resolves F→E back to I. In this view, the “deep structure” is I, and the surface chords are harmonizations of lines that move around stable tones. B) Functional reading Here you treat each chord as having a primary role in a tonal syntax: C (I): Tonic (T) — establishes center G (V): Dominant (D) — creates expectation of return to tonic Am (vi): Tonic substitute (Tsub) — deceptive resolution, partial fulfillment F (IV): Predominant/Subdominant (PD) — prepares return to tonic, soft cadence IV→I So the loop becomes: T → D → Tsub → PD → (back to T) Both are legitimate; they answer different questions: - Prolongation reading explains why it can loop smoothly and support simple melodies: lots of common tones and stepwise lines. - Functional reading explains why it still has a sense of movement: dominant energy, deceptive deflection, and return. If you want to connect them, you can say: the loop has functional labels on the surface, but its voice-leading is so parsimonious that it can also be heard as tonic prolongation.
Could you broaden this to other keys and show the general pattern of voice-leading? Like, is there a “template” in scale degrees that always works?

Yes. In any major key, I–V–vi–IV has a consistent scale-degree template. If the key is major with scale degrees 1–7, the chord roots are: I: 1 V: 5 vi: 6 IV: 4 Chord tones (as scale degrees in major): - I: 1–3–5 - V: 5–7–2 - vi: 6–1–3 - IV: 4–6–1 A voice-leading template that generalizes well is the three-line framework we built: Line A (leading tone resolution around V→vi): - 1 → 7 → 1 → 1 → 1 This is: scale degree 7 resolves to 1 even when V goes to vi. Line B (2–3 and 4–3 resolutions): - 3 → 2 → 3 → 4 → 3 This gives the sense of dominant-related 2→3 and plagal-related 4→3 at the seam. Line C (5→6 lift at the deceptive move): - 5 → 5 → 6 → 6 → 5 This captures the characteristic “upward step” from V to vi (root motion 5→6). These lines are diatonic in any major key and can be voiced into close-position triads using the same inversion pattern: I (root) → V6 → vi6 → IV64 → I In scale-degree bass terms (if you take the lowest voice from those voicings): - 1 → 7 → 1 → 1 → 1 which is not a “root bass” progression but a smooth voice-leading bass. If you want a more “rooted” template while still smooth: - Put roots in bass (1–5–6–4) and then choose upper voices to move mostly by step using common tones: - Keep scale degree 1 as a common tone across I→vi→IV. - Use 7→1 resolution across V→vi. - Use 4→3 across IV→I. So yes: there are reusable scale-degree motions that make the progression work in any key, and they’re largely independent of absolute pitch. If you name a key (say E major or Ab major), I can spell out the chords and show the same inversion chain and inner-voice lines in that key.
Try it in E major. Give the chords and the smooth inversion chain analogous to C–G/B–Am/C–F/C.

In E major, the diatonic scale is: E F# G# A B C# D#. I–V–vi–IV becomes: - I: E major (E–G#–B) - V: B major (B–D#–F#) - vi: C# minor (C#–E–G#) - IV: A major (A–C#–E) Now the analogous smooth inversion chain (I root → V6 → vi6 → IV64 → I): 1) E major (root position close): E–G#–B 2) B major in first inversion (V6): D#–F#–B Voice-leading from E–G#–B: - B stays common (B→B) - G#→F# (step down) - E→D# (step down) So you get D#–F#–B. 3) C# minor in first inversion (vi6): E–G#–C# From D#–F#–B: - D#→E (step up) - F#→G# (step up) - B→C# (step up) Result: E–G#–C#. 4) A major in second inversion (IV64): E–A–C# From E–G#–C#: - E stays E (common) - C# stays C# (common) - G#→A (step up) Result: E–A–C#. 5) Back to E major: E–G#–B From E–A–C#: - E stays E (common) - A→G# (step down) - C#→B (step down) Result: E–G#–B. So the chain is: E (E–G#–B) B/D# (D#–F#–B) C#m/E (E–G#–C#) A/E (E–A–C#) → E (E–G#–B) Exactly parallel to the C-major example: stepwise motion, common tones, and a very smooth loop.
Nice. Shifting gears a bit: sometimes people say the progression “works” because it’s basically a descending-fifths sequence, but it isn’t. Is there any sequence logic here?

It’s not a pure circle-of-fifths (descending fifths) sequence, but it contains partial sequential logic and familiar root motions that resemble pieces of common sequences. Root motion in I–V–vi–IV (in C): C→G→A→F Intervals: - C→G: up a 5th (or down a 4th) — a strong functional move (tonic to dominant). - G→A: up a 2nd — a stepwise “lift,” common in deceptive patterns. - A→F: down a 3rd — common in diatonic progressions (vi to IV). - F→C: down a 4th (if you close the loop) — plagal motion. So it mixes: - a strong fifth relation (I→V), - stepwise motion (V→vi), - third relation (vi→IV), - fourth relation (IV→I). Sequence logic is more evident if you look at the smooth inversion chain, where voices move in parallel stepwise motion for a moment: C–E–G → B–D–G → C–E–A That’s a kind of “parallel” or “planing-like” motion in diatonic space: two voices go down then up, etc. Also, you can interpret part of it as a “descending-third” framework if you rotate it: vi–IV–I–V (Am–F–C–G) has roots A→F→C→G, which are descending thirds then a fourth: - A→F: down a 3rd - F→C: down a 4th - C→G: down a 4th Not a strict sequence, but it has a coherent pattern of related roots. Why it feels sequence-like anyway: - The chords are all diatonic and closely related. - The voice-leading can be highly regular (stepwise). - Repetition in a loop creates the perceptual effect of sequence even when the interval pattern isn’t a classical sequence. So: not a circle-of-fifths sequence, but it borrows some of the “predictability” and “directional cues” that sequences provide—especially through V’s dominant function and the consistent voice-leading.
Another thing: people talk about “four-chord loops” as if they’re harmonically static. But clearly there’s motion. How do you describe harmonic motion in a loop without relying on cadences?

You can describe harmonic motion in loops using concepts like tension curves, stability hierarchy, and voice-leading directedness rather than phrase-ending cadences. Here are some useful descriptors: 1) Stability vs. instability (local harmonic gravity) Rank chords by how stable they feel relative to a center: - Tonic (I) is most stable. - Tonic substitutes (vi, iii) can be fairly stable. - Predominants (IV, ii) are intermediate: they often “lean” somewhere. - Dominant (V, vii°) is most unstable: it points strongly away from itself. Then describe the loop as cycling through stability levels: I (stable) → V (unstable) → vi (stable-ish) → IV (intermediate) → I (stable) That’s harmonic motion as a repeating stability waveform. 2) Directed voice-leading (tendency tones) without closure Even if nothing “ends,” certain tones still resolve: - B→C across V→vi - F→E across IV→I - D→E or D→C depending on voicing So the ear experiences micro-resolutions each chord change. Motion is the accumulation of these resolutions, not a single final cadence. 3) Loop seam behavior (turnaround strength) Describe how strongly the last chord sets up the first: - IV→I = gentle reset - V→I = strong reset - V→vi = deceptive reset This helps explain why two loops with the same chords but different ordering feel different. 4) Harmonic rhythm and grouping Even in a constant loop, the ear can group chords into larger units: - [I–V] as a “departure” - [vi–IV] as a “return preparation” or in the rotated loop: - [vi–IV] as a “minor region” - [I–V] as a “major/dominant region” Motion is then the alternation of regions. 5) Tonal center maintenance A loop can be “in motion” while still centric: - Many loops keep the center stable (C is always implied). - Motion happens as changing coloration around that center. So you can talk about harmonic motion as: - cyclic tension-release, - voice-leading resolution events, - turnaround strength, - and region alternation, without needing a cadence that ends the section.
Can we look at some common substitutions within I–V–vi–IV that keep the overall character? Like, what can replace IV, what can replace V, etc.?

Sure—here are common diatonic and near-diatonic substitutions that preserve much of the progression’s logic. I’ll describe in C major for concreteness. Original: C (I) – G (V) – Am (vi) – F (IV) 1) Substitute for IV (F) a) ii (Dm) as predominant substitute - C – G – Am – Dm Roman: I – V – vi – ii Effect: more “pre-dominant” pull because ii often leads strongly to V (if you extend). It can make the loop feel a bit more functional and less plagal. b) IV6 or IVadd6 / IVmaj7 as color variants - Keep F but change voicing/extensions: F/A, F6, Fmaj7. Effect: preserves the role but alters smoothness and tension. c) bVII (Bb) as modal-mixture substitute (borrowed from C Mixolydian) - C – G – Am – Bb Effect: more modal/rock flavor, weaker classical dominant logic, strong stepwise root motion Bb→C at seam. 2) Substitute for V (G) a) iii (Em) as a softer dominant substitute - C – Em – Am – F Roman: I – iii – vi – IV Effect: reduces dominant tension; becomes a more “tonic-prolonging” loop (all chords share lots of tones). Still extremely common. b) Vsus4 (Gsus4) or V7 - C – Gsus4–G – Am – F (or simply G7) Effect: strengthens tendency-tone resolutions while keeping the same harmonic position. c) bVII (Bb) as dominant-area substitute (modal) - C – Bb – Am – F Effect: changes functional grammar but can keep loop vibe; more “mixolydian” color. 3) Substitute for vi (Am) a) iii (Em) or I6 (C/E) as tonic substitutes - C – G – Em – F or C – G – C/E – F Effect: keeps common tones and smoothness; can sound more “major” overall. b) vi7 or vi add9 as color - Am7, Am(add9): adds tones that connect melodically. 4) Substitute for I (C) a) vi (Am) as relative-minor start (rotation) - Am – G – F – C (a common rotation/variant) Effect: changes centricity perception. b) I6 (C/E) to create stepwise bass - C/E – G – Am – F Effect: smoother bass line and more linear feel. General substitution rule of thumb: - Replace a chord with another chord that shares 2 common tones (or at least 1 strong common tone) and has similar function (tonic-ish, predominant-ish, dominant-ish). That’s why iii can replace I or V-ish roles sometimes, and ii can replace IV. If you tell me which chord you want to substitute and whether you want to keep it diatonic, I can propose 3–5 options and explain their function and voice-leading.
I’m curious about the idea that iii can substitute for V. In classical theory, iii isn’t dominant. Why does I–iii–vi–IV still feel coherent?

You’re right: in common-practice functional harmony, iii is not a dominant-function chord; it’s often weak and used as a passing harmony. But in loop-based pop contexts, coherence often comes more from voice-leading and shared tones than from strict dominant function. In C major, iii is Em (E–G–B). Compare it to V (G–B–D): - They share G and B (two common tones). - The difference is D (in V) vs E (in iii). So replacing G with Em keeps much of the “dominant area” pitch content (especially the leading tone B), but softens the dominant pull because: - Em doesn’t have scale degree 2 (D) as a chord tone, so you lose some of the V’s characteristic sound. - The root E doesn’t have the same dominant-to-tonic root relationship to C. Why it still works: 1) Leading tone is still present Em contains B (scale degree 7), which still wants to resolve to C. So you retain a key tendency tone. 2) Stepwise root motion becomes smoother C→Em is a third relation (roots C→E), often perceived as smooth and consonant in pop harmony. Then Em→Am is also a fourth relation (E→A), which is strong in its own right. So you get a different but still coherent root-motion path: C → Em → Am → F → C Roots: 1 → 3 → 6 → 4 → 1 3) Tonic-prolongation increases C (I), Em (iii), and Am (vi) all share two tones with one another in various pairings. This creates a “tonic field” feel: a cluster of closely related chords rather than a tonic–dominant drama. 4) Melody often supplies the missing dominant function Even if the harmony lacks a strong V, the melody can emphasize scale degrees 2 and 7 in ways that imply dominant pull. For example, if the melody hits D over Em (as a 7th), that moment can sound like a dominant-ish tension even though the chord is iii. So I–iii–vi–IV is coherent because it preserves: - diatonic consistency, - strong common-tone networks, - and key tendency tones (especially 7), even while reducing classical dominant function.
That makes sense. Could you talk about “tonal ambiguity” in these loops? Like, when does the loop stop sounding clearly major and start sounding ambiguous between relative major/minor?

Tonal ambiguity between relative major and minor emerges when the cues that normally establish a single tonic are weakened or balanced. For the shared collection C major / A minor, ambiguity is common because the pitch set is identical, so you rely on contextual cues. Key factors that increase ambiguity: 1) Starting chord and repetition If the loop starts on vi (Am) and repeats without a strong cadence to C, A can start to feel like the center. - vi–IV–I–V (Am–F–C–G) is a prime example. 2) Lack of strong dominant-to-tonic cadence for one center - To confirm C major, V→I (G→C) is strong, especially if B→C is highlighted. - To confirm A minor in tonal (not modal) terms, you’d expect E→Am with G#→A. If neither of these cadential confirmations is emphasized, the center can remain ambiguous. 3) Melody’s focal pitches If the melody repeatedly lands on A and E (tonic and fifth of A minor) at structurally important moments, A becomes a plausible tonic. If it lands on C and G or resolves B→C decisively, C becomes more plausible. 4) Harmonic rhythm emphasis If the chord that feels most “restful” occurs on the strongest points of the loop, that chord tends to be heard as tonic. A loop that makes C appear in a weaker position can reduce C’s dominance. 5) Use of scale degree 6 and 3 as “pivot” tones C major and A minor share many tones; the most telling difference is the role of A and C as 1 vs 6 (or 1 vs 3). - If A is treated as scale degree 1 (stable final), it suggests A minor. - If A is treated as scale degree 6 (color tone), it suggests C major. 6) Absence of chromatic leading tone in minor If there’s no G#, A minor is more modal (Aeolian) and can coexist ambiguously with C major because neither is strongly confirmed by leading-tone dominants. So the loop becomes ambiguous when: - it avoids strong cadential confirmation, - rotates chords so vi feels structurally primary, - and uses melody/phrase emphasis that doesn’t clearly privilege C over A. If you give an example of a chord order and a typical melodic landing note at the loop seam, I can tell you which tonic is more likely to be perceived and why.
How would you notate that ambiguity analytically? Roman numerals assume a key. Is there a better system?

Roman numerals do assume a reference key, so for ambiguous loops analysts often use one of these approaches: 1) Dual Roman numeral analysis (two readings) Write the same chords with two possible key centers. Example: Am–F–C–G - In C major: vi–IV–I–V - In A minor (natural minor/Aeolian): i–VI–III–VII Both are correct relative to their assumed centers. Presenting both acknowledges ambiguity. 2) Nashville numbers / scale-degree chords without committing to major/minor You can label chords by scale-degree root relative to a pitch center but leave mode unspecified, or use “1–5–6–4” as a root pattern in major and “3–7–1–5” relative to minor, etc. This can still imply a center, though. 3) “Chord-loop” labels (root motion pattern) Just label the root sequence in absolute terms (C–G–Am–F) and discuss centricity separately. This avoids forcing function. 4) Function labels without Roman numerals Use categories like: T (tonic area), PD (predominant), D (dominant), TS (tonic substitute) But you can also admit multiple plausible centers: - “C-centric with tonic-substitute vi” - or “A-centric Aeolian with III as major-color chord” 5) Pitch-centric or modal analysis Describe it as “A Aeolian centric” if A is the center, without implying harmonic-minor functional syntax. Then you can label chords as i–VII–VI–VII-ish, but in Aeolian you might not want to call VII “dominant”; you’d call it “subtonic major” chord. In practice, a hybrid is clearest: - give the chord loop in letter names, - give two Roman numeral readings, - then justify which center is stronger with musical evidence (melody endpoints, seam behavior, cadential cues). If you want, we can take one loop and write both analyses, then list the specific cues that would push it toward one or the other.
Let’s do that for Am–F–C–G. Give both analyses and the cues that would decide.

Chords: Am – F – C – G Analysis 1: In C major - Am = vi - F = IV - C = I - G = V So: vi–IV–I–V Cues favoring C major as tonic: - Melodic resolution to C (scale degree 1 of C major) at phrase endings. - Strong emphasis on G→C motion (V→I), especially at structural points (even though in this loop G goes back to Am at the seam; if larger phrases land on C, that still supports C). - Prominent B→C resolution in melody or inner voices. - Harmonic emphasis on C chord (longer duration, metrically strong placement, or repeated arrival feel). - Use of D leading to G and then to C (functional dominant preparation), even if only implied. Analysis 2: In A minor (natural minor / Aeolian) - Am = i - F = VI - C = III - G = VII (subtonic major) So: i–VI–III–VII Cues favoring A as center (A Aeolian / A minor centric): - Melodic resolution to A at phrase endings; A treated as final/most stable pitch. - Loop begins on Am and returns there repeatedly; if the seam (G→Am) feels like “coming home,” that’s a strong centric cue. - Lack of strong C-major cadential behavior (e.g., melody avoids strong B→C resolution, or C chord doesn’t get special emphasis). - Modal melodic behavior: frequent use of G natural as stable scale degree 7 of Aeolian (subtonic), without introducing G#. - If the melody treats C as a color tone (b3 in A minor) rather than as tonic. Deciding cues (most decisive): - Phrase endings: where do phrases land harmonically and melodically? - Leading-tone behavior: do you hear B→C as a key resolution (C major) or is it not highlighted? - Presence/absence of E major/E7 (with G#): if E7→Am appears anywhere, that strongly supports A minor as tonicized/tonic. - “Seam feeling”: does G→Am feel like reset/home (A center) or like a deceptive continuation away from C (C center)? Often, the best conclusion is conditional: the loop is compatible with either center, and the melody and phrase structure decide.
I want to understand the progression’s “hookiness” from a cognitive perspective. Without talking about subjective feelings, can you explain why it’s easy to remember?

From a cognitive/music-perception perspective, it’s memorable because it balances predictability with slight surprise, uses simple categorical relationships, and forms a tight repeating pattern with strong internal coherence. Key factors: 1) Chunking into a short, repeating unit A four-chord loop is a manageable-sized “chunk.” Repetition reinforces memory quickly, and the loop length is short enough to be held in working memory. 2) High schematicity (matches common tonal schemas) Listeners internalize common tonal patterns: - tonic establishing, - dominant tension, - resolution (or quasi-resolution), - return. I–V–vi–IV fits many of those expectations. 3) Predictability with one twist I→V is highly predictable in major tonality. V→vi is a common deceptive move: unexpected but still rule-governed and smooth. That combination (expected step + mild violation) is known to enhance attention and recall: it’s not random, but it’s not completely redundant either. 4) Smooth voice-leading reduces processing load Because many voices can move by step or hold common tones, the auditory system can track continuity. Patterns with minimal discontinuity are easier to encode and anticipate. 5) Diatonic simplicity reduces ambiguity at the surface All chords are diatonic triads in the key; there are no complex alterations required. That makes categorization easier: each chord belongs clearly to the same pitch collection. 6) Strong bass/root pattern (if roots are emphasized) Even abstractly, the root sequence 1–5–6–4 is distinctive and easy to internalize because it mixes: - a large leap (1→5), - a step (5→6), - a moderate leap (6→4), creating a recognizable contour. 7) Multiple redundant cues The progression provides redundancy: - functional cues (dominant presence), - voice-leading cues (tendency-tone resolutions), - common-tone anchors (scale degree 1 present in 3 of 4 chords), all pointing to a coherent tonal space. Redundant cues improve learnability. So it’s memorable because it’s a compact loop with strong internal logic, easy-to-track voice-leading, and one salient “ruleful surprise” (the deceptive move), all within a familiar diatonic framework.
Could you formalize the “predictability with surprise” aspect using expectation theory? Like, is V–vi a high-probability event in pop?

Using expectation theory (in the broad sense—statistical learning plus schematic expectations), you can describe V–vi as an event that’s less probable than V–I but still sufficiently common to be recognized as a legitimate continuation. A way to formalize it conceptually: 1) Hierarchical expectation: local vs. global - Locally, after V, the strongest tonal expectation in a major key is I (authentic resolution). - But globally, in loop-based pop schemas, V→vi is a recognized alternative resolution pattern because it supports continuous looping and avoids full closure. So the expectancy depends on which schema the listener is using: - common-practice cadence schema: V→I expected - pop loop schema: V→vi plausible/expected 2) Information content and surprise In information-theoretic terms, surprise relates to -log P(event | context). - If V→I is more probable than V→vi in a given listener’s internal model, V→vi carries higher information content. - But if V→vi still has moderate probability (not extremely low), it’s surprising enough to be salient but not so surprising as to be rejected. 3) Why V→vi remains coherent despite surprise Because the event is supported by strong voice-leading constraints: - Leading tone (7) can still resolve to 1 (B→C in C major) even when harmony goes to vi. This reduces perceived violation: the “surface” expectation (tendency-tone resolution) is met even if the “root” expectation (V→I) is not. 4) Pop corpus perspective (qualitative) In many pop styles, deceptive resolutions and relative-minor shifts are common enough that listeners have learned them as part of the vocabulary. So V→vi can be high-probability within that style model. 5) The loop’s recurrence reduces surprise over time Even if the first occurrence is surprising, repetition quickly trains the listener: after one cycle, V→vi becomes expected within that specific piece’s local statistics. So you can say: - First pass: V→vi has moderate surprise relative to a generic tonal model. - After repetition: V→vi becomes highly expected in the piece-specific model. That dynamic (initial salience, then learned predictability) is a strong recipe for memorability.
Can you show how the same four chords can support different tonal centers depending on melodic emphasis, using just scale-degree examples?

Yes. Take the chord set {C, G, Am, F} (I, V, vi, IV in C major). Depending on which pitch the melody treats as “final” and how it resolves tendency tones, you can imply different centers. I’ll show three centers: C (Ionian), A (Aeolian), and G (Mixolydian-ish) as perceptual centers, using scale degrees relative to each center. 1) C as center (C Ionian) Chords: C (I), G (V), Am (vi), F (IV) Melodic emphasis strategy: - Frequent phrase endpoints on C (scale degree 1). - Use B→C as a key resolution gesture. Example melody degrees in C: 3–2–1 (E–D–C) at phrase ends, or 7–1 (B–C). 2) A as center (A Aeolian) Same chords reinterpreted: Am (i), F (VI), C (III), G (VII) Melodic emphasis strategy: - Phrase endpoints on A (scale degree 1 of A). - Use G→A as a characteristic modal resolution (subtonic to tonic) rather than G#→A. - Emphasize C as b3 (scale degree 3 in A minor), giving minor-mode identity. Example melody degrees in A: 3–2–1 (C–B–A) or 7–1 (G–A) at phrase ends. 3) G as center (G Mixolydian-ish, using same collection) Reinterpretation: G (I), F (bVII), C (IV), Am (ii) if you reorder/weight them; even with the same set you can imply G centricity if G is emphasized. Melodic emphasis strategy: - Phrase endpoints on G. - Use F natural prominently (b7 in G mixolydian). - Avoid strong pull to C as final; treat C as IV. Example melody degrees in G: 1–b7–1 (G–F–G) or 2–1 (A–G). The key idea: the chords provide a pitch collection and several stable triads. The melody determines centricity by: - where it resolves, - which tendency tones are highlighted, - and which scale degrees recur at structurally important points.
That’s clear. Could you explain why IV–I (plagal motion) feels like a “reset” in these loops even though it’s not as strong as V–I?

IV–I works as a reset because it shares tones with I and provides a stable downward step in tendency-like motion (4→3 or 6→5) even without the leading-tone drive of V–I. In C major, F→C: 1) Common tones and minimal change F major (F–A–C) shares C with C major (C–E–G). With certain voicings, you can keep C fixed and move the other tones by step: - F→E - A→G That makes the change smooth and “settling.” 2) Resolution-like voice-leading Even though IV doesn’t contain the leading tone B, it contains scale degree 4 (F), which often resolves to 3 (E) in the tonic context (a classic 4–3 motion). So IV→I can include a clear resolution gesture: F→E. 3) Predominant to tonic in a loop context In functional terms, IV is often predominant. Predominant→tonic is not a cadence in classical syntax (it skips the dominant), but in a loop it can still mark a return to stability because I is the most stable chord. The ear doesn’t require dominant preparation every time to recognize tonic as “home.” 4) Loop seam reinforcement If IV→I occurs at the seam (end of loop to beginning), the repetition itself amplifies the sense of reset. The listener learns that IV is the pre-seam chord and I is the post-seam chord; that association becomes a structural cue. 5) Contrast with V–I V–I has: - leading tone 7→1 - strong root motion 5→1 - often a tritone resolution if V7 is used IV–I lacks those, so it’s softer. But softness can be an advantage in loops: it resets without sounding like a full stop, making continuous repetition feel natural. So IV–I is an effective “reset” because it’s: - smooth (common tones, stepwise motion), - stable (arrives on tonic), - and structurally reinforced by repetition.
Could you discuss how adding sevenths changes the progression? Like, turning it into Imaj7–V7–vi7–IVmaj7 or something. What does that do to the voice-leading?

Adding sevenths increases the density of tendency tones and creates more stepwise resolution opportunities, often making the loop feel more linear and connected. In C major, one common extension set is: Cmaj7 – G7 – Am7 – Fmaj7 Chords: - Cmaj7: C–E–G–B - G7: G–B–D–F - Am7: A–C–E–G - Fmaj7: F–A–C–E What changes: 1) More common tones between adjacent chords Compare triads vs sevenths: - Cmaj7 → G7 shares B (common) and can share G depending on voicing. - G7 → Am7 shares G (common) and can move other tones stepwise. - Am7 → Fmaj7 shares A and C and E? Actually Am7 has A–C–E–G; Fmaj7 has F–A–C–E: three common tones A, C, E. That’s huge. - Fmaj7 → Cmaj7 shares C and E and G? Fmaj7 has F–A–C–E; Cmaj7 has C–E–G–B: two common tones (C, E). So the loop becomes extremely “sticky” via common tones. 2) Clear tendency-tone resolutions appear - In G7, the 7th (F) tends to resolve down to E when moving to C (or to E in Am7 as well). In this loop, G7 goes to Am7, so F→E can still happen because E is in Am7. Again, a deceptive-like satisfaction: the 7th resolves as expected even though the chord doesn’t go to I. - In Cmaj7, the major 7th (B) tends to resolve up to C, but if the chord moves away, you can treat B as a common tone into G7 (where B is the 3rd). That reinterprets the tendency tone as stable, which is a smooth kind of “deferral.” 3) Stepwise inner lines become even more pronounced A classic voice-leading chain for these seventh chords (one possible set of lines) might include: - B (in Cmaj7) stays as B (in G7), then resolves to C (in Am7), then stays as C (in Fmaj7). - F (in G7) resolves to E (in Am7), and E is common into Fmaj7 and then into Cmaj7. So you get multiple simultaneous semitone/whole-step resolutions, which strengthens continuity. 4) The chords’ functions can feel more “tonal-jazz” even in diatonic context Sevenths make dominant chords more explicitly dominant (G7 has the tritone B–F). Even if it doesn’t resolve to I, the internal voice-leading of that tritone can still resolve partially: - F→E - B can go to C (if desired) So the ear hears functional tension and release embedded within the loop. 5) Potential downside: increased dissonance management Major 7ths (in Imaj7, IVmaj7) are mild dissonances that often want careful melodic alignment. But because these are common sonorities, many melodies can simply treat those chord tones as color. So overall: sevenths increase connectivity and directed motion via additional tendency tones and common tones, making the loop smoother and sometimes more sophisticated harmonically.
Could you spell out one clean four-voice voice-leading solution for Cmaj7–G7–Am7–Fmaj7 with minimal motion?

Yes. Let’s pick a close-position-ish four-voice set and minimize motion between chords. I’ll list voices as (top to bottom) S, A, T, B just as registral slots. Chord 1: Cmaj7 (C–E–G–B) Choose: S=B, A=G, T=E, B=C So: (S) B, (A) G, (T) E, (B) C Chord 2: G7 (G–B–D–F) We want minimal motion from B–G–E–C. A smooth target: - Keep B common: S stays B (B is 3rd of G7). - Keep G common: A stays G (root of G7). - Move E down to D: T E→D (step). - Move C down to B? But B is already taken; instead move bass C up to D? That would give D in bass, making it G7/D (2nd inversion). Let’s see: If B voice stays B and A stays G and T goes to D, remaining chord tone needed is F. Where can F come from with minimal motion? - Bass C→B is step down but duplicates B. - Bass C→D is step up and gives D (already present). - Bass C→F is a leap (4th). Alternative: change voicing slightly by moving the top B to F (B→F is tritone, too big). Better: start Cmaj7 in a different inversion to make the move easier. Let’s redo with a smarter starting voicing that leads cleanly. Try Cmaj7 as: (S) G, (A) E, (T) C, (B) B This is Cmaj7 in 3rd inversion (B in bass): B–C–E–G. Now to G7 (G–B–D–F): From B–C–E–G: - Keep B common in bass: B stays B (now 3rd of G7 in bass: G7/B). - Move C down to B? would duplicate; instead move C up to D (step): T C→D. - Move E down to D? would duplicate; instead E→F (step up): A E→F. - Keep G common: S G stays G. Result: (B) B, (T) D, (A) F, (S) G = B–D–F–G, which is G7 in 1st inversion (G7/B) with all chord tones present. Motions: all step or common tone. Chord 3: Am7 (A–C–E–G) From B–D–F–G: We want A–C–E–G. - Keep G common: S stays G. - Move F down to E: A F→E (step). - Move D up to E or down to C: choose D→C (step down) to avoid duplicating E: T D→C. - Move bass B up to C or down to A: choose B→A (step down) for a strong resolution feel (leading tone to A if hearing A as local center, or just stepwise): B B→A. Result: (B) A, (T) C, (A) E, (S) G = A–C–E–G (Am7 root position). Great: all step/common tone. Chord 4: Fmaj7 (F–A–C–E) From A–C–E–G: We want F–A–C–E. - Keep A common in bass: B stays A (Fmaj7/A = 1st inversion). - Keep C common: T stays C. - Keep E common: A stays E. - Move G down to F: S G→F (step). Result: (B) A, (T) C, (A) E, (S) F = A–C–E–F, which is Fmaj7 in 1st inversion (Fmaj7/A). Again minimal motion. Then back to Cmaj7: from A–C–E–F to B–C–E–G (our original Cmaj7 voicing): - Bass A→B (step up) - T C stays C - A E stays E - S F→G (step up) Everything is stepwise or common tone. So the full loop voicing is: Cmaj7/B: B–C–E–G G7/B: B–D–F–G Am7: A–C–E–G Fmaj7/A: A–C–E–F → back This is a very parsimonious four-voice solution.
Great. Now, can we address a common claim: that I–V–vi–IV is “sad” because it includes vi. But that seems oversimplified. How would you critique that claim purely musically?

Musically, the claim is oversimplified because chord quality alone doesn’t determine the overall character, and vi in a major key often functions as a closely related tonic substitute rather than a wholesale shift to minor. A critique on purely musical grounds: 1) vi is diatonic and closely related to I In major, vi shares two chord tones with I (1 and 3 of the key). In C: - I: C–E–G - vi: A–C–E So moving to vi doesn’t necessarily create a stark contrast; it can feel like a smooth reinterpretation of the same pitch material. 2) The progression is mostly major triads I, V, IV are major. Only vi is minor. If chord quality were the main determinant, the overall sonority is still heavily major. 3) Context decides: melody and emphasis If the melody emphasizes chord tones that highlight the minor third of vi (C in Am as the 3rd relative to A) and treats A as a focal pitch, you may perceive more minor coloration. But if the melody stays centered on C and E and treats Am as passing, vi can sound like a gentle detour rather than a defining “minor mood.” 4) V→vi is deceptive, not simply “minor appears” The vi chord is significant because it is the deceptive resolution of V. The effect comes from *expectation and deflection*, not merely from the chord being minor. 5) Many other progressions contain vi without the same effect I–vi–IV–V is also common; it contains vi but has a strong V→I seam, often reading as more straightforwardly major. So vi alone doesn’t explain the perceived quality. 6) Harmonic rhythm and voicing matter If vi is emphasized (longer duration, stronger position, bass emphasis), it contributes more to the overall color. If it’s brief or inverted, it may not. So a better musical explanation is: - The progression’s distinctive feature is the dominant’s redirected resolution (V→vi) combined with smooth voice-leading and a loopable cadence seam. The minor chord contributes contrast, but it’s not the sole or even primary cause.
I like that. Could you also discuss how melody can “override” harmony? Like, can a melody make I–V–vi–IV sound more like C major or more like A minor even if chords stay fixed?

Yes. Melody can strongly bias perceived center and mode because listeners often track melodic resolution and focal pitches more than chord labels, especially in repetitive harmonic contexts. With C–G–Am–F fixed, melody can steer perception: 1) Making it strongly C major (C-centric) Strategies: - End phrases on C over the C chord (scale degree 1). - Use B as leading tone resolving to C (7→1) at prominent moments. - Emphasize E as stable (scale degree 3) over I and vi, reinforcing major quality. - Treat A as scale degree 6 (a color tone) rather than as a goal. Example melodic gestures (scale degrees in C): - 7–1 (B–C) at phrase ends - 2–1 (D–C) or 3–2–1 (E–D–C) - Frequent resting on 1 or 3 2) Making it feel A minor / A Aeolian centric (without changing chords) Strategies: - Begin and/or end phrases on A, especially over Am. - Use G natural as a stable “subtonic” (b7 of A), resolving G→A often. - Emphasize C as a stable pitch (b3 in A minor), and E as 5. - Avoid strong B→C cadential emphasis that would point to C as tonic; treat B as passing. Example melodic gestures (scale degrees in A Aeolian): - b7–1 (G–A) as a recurring resolution - 3–2–1 (C–B–A) at phrase ends - Frequent resting on 1 (A) and 3 (C) 3) Why this works with fixed chords Because the chord set supports both centers reasonably well: - C chord is III in A minor (still stable). - G chord is VII in A minor (common in Aeolian). - F chord is VI in A minor. So the harmony doesn’t contradict A Aeolian; it’s a standard Aeolian loop. Conversely, in C major the same chords are diatonic and functional. So the melody can “override” by: - determining where closure is perceived (final tones), - highlighting leading-tone vs subtonic behavior, - and selecting which chord tones feel like stable endpoints.
Could you give two short 8-note melodies (as scale degrees) over C–G–Am–F that push strongly toward C major vs toward A minor?

Yes. I’ll assume 2 notes per chord (8 notes total over 4 chords), and I’ll give scale degrees in the C-major system first, then interpret the A-minor-leaning one in A terms as well. A) Melody pushing toward C major (C-centric) Goal: emphasize B→C and land on C at the end. Over C: 3–2 (E–D) Over G: 7–1? But 1 over G is C (a 4th/11th) which can be color; better: 7–2 (B–D) to fit G. Over Am: 1–3 (C–E) fits Am well (3rd and 5th). Over F: 2–1 (D–C), ending on C (5th of F) which can then resolve to C at loop restart. So scale degrees in C: 3–2 | 7–2 | 1–3 | 2–1 Notes: E–D | B–D | C–E | D–C This melody includes the leading tone B and points to C as an endpoint. B) Melody pushing toward A minor / A Aeolian centric Goal: emphasize G→A and land on A at the end, avoid strong B→C closure. We’ll still write in C-major scale degrees for consistency, but center A: - A is scale degree 6 in C. - G is scale degree 5 in C. Over C: 6–5 (A–G) (A is 6 of C, but can feel like 1 of A; both notes are in C chord as 6 and 5? Actually A is not in C triad; it’s a 6th color. G is chord tone.) Over G: 5–6 (G–A) (A is a 9th over G; common pop color) Over Am: 6–1 (A–C) (both chord tones of Am: root and 3rd) Over F: 1–6 (C–A) (both chord tones of F: 5th and 3rd), ending on A. So in C degrees: 6–5 | 5–6 | 6–1 | 1–6 Notes: A–G | G–A | A–C | C–A Interpreted in A Aeolian degrees (A=1): A–G is 1–b7 G–A is b7–1 A–C is 1–b3 C–A is b3–1 That’s a very strong Aeolian-centric pattern: it repeatedly resolves b7→1 and ends on 1 (A). These are minimal examples, but they show how melodic endpoints and characteristic resolutions can bias centricity even over the same chords.
That’s exactly what I wanted. Now, could you explain why vi–IV–I–V (the rotation) is sometimes called the “pop-punk progression” and how its harmonic logic differs from I–V–vi–IV?

The label “pop-punk progression” is more of a genre association than a theoretical category, but vi–IV–I–V (in C: Am–F–C–G) has a harmonic logic that differs from I–V–vi–IV mainly in where the strongest motions occur and what the loop seam does. 1) Seam behavior: deceptive vs dominant reset - vi–IV–I–V loops back with V→vi at the seam (G→Am). That’s a deceptive-type reset in C-major terms. - I–V–vi–IV loops back with IV→I (F→C), a plagal reset. So in vi–IV–I–V, each cycle ends on V, which sets up a strong expectation. But instead of resolving to I, it resets to vi. That creates a repeating “set up → sidestep” pattern at the seam. 2) Internal arrival: IV→I happens mid-loop In vi–IV–I–V, the IV→I move (F→C) happens as chords 2→3. That can feel like a stable internal arrival, after which the harmony ramps toward V again. In I–V–vi–IV, IV→I is at the seam, so the arrival aligns with the loop restart. 3) Center perception Starting on vi makes it easier for the ear to treat the relative minor as a center, especially if the melody cooperates. That gives the loop a different centric “default” than starting on I. 4) Tension distribution - I–V–vi–IV: tension appears early (V at chord 2), then released deceptively. - vi–IV–I–V: tension is delayed (V at chord 4), creating a build to the end of the loop. 5) Why it’s genre-sticky A loop that ends on V and repeats tends to create continuous forward drive because the end is “open.” That’s useful in styles that favor persistent momentum. Also, the chord set is diatonic, easy to cycle, and supports strong melodic hooks. So the harmonic logic difference is mostly about: - where the dominant appears, - how the loop resets, - and which chord is treated as the structural starting point.
Last big question: if you had to summarize the progression’s power in one technical explanation, what would it be?

I–V–vi–IV is powerful because it combines strong tonal schema cues (tonic and dominant presence) with exceptionally parsimonious voice-leading, including a deceptive dominant resolution that satisfies key tendency-tone expectations (like 7→1) while avoiding full closure—making it both directionally coherent and endlessly loopable.
